figures.gloss: Boundless Time as the neuter, primordial godhead who existed before all things and sacrificed for a thousand years to obtain a son — becoming father of both Ohrmazd and Ahriman, then withdrawing behind the battle he had seeded.
figures.facet: Dualism's attempt to heal itself into monism: one parent set above the warring twins, so that good and evil become two-from-one rather than two co-eternals — at the price of making Time, not Goodness, ultimate.
figures.provenance: Eznik of Kolb, Refutation of the Sects (c. 440s); Theodore bar Konai, Scholion; Zaehner, Zurvan: A Zoroastrian Dilemma (1955). Zurvan appears in the Avesta only as a minor time-divinity — the exalted Zurvan is the heresy's move.

figures.gloss: At the end of the thousand-year sacrifice Zurvan wavered — 'of what use is my offering?' — and from that single doubt Ahriman was conceived, while Ohrmazd was conceived of the sacrifice itself; Ahriman tore his way out of the womb first to claim the promised kingship.
figures.facet: The rarest answer to the problem of evil: evil born not from a rival principle nor a fallen creature but from a moment of hesitation INSIDE the one god — doubt itself as the origin of the Adversary.
figures.provenance: Eznik of Kolb's Armenian account, the fullest surviving version of the myth; widely read as exegesis of Yasna 30.3, where the two primal Spirits are called 'twins' without a named parent.

figures.gloss: Bound by his own vow to give the firstborn the kingdom, Zurvan grants Ahriman rulership of the world for a fixed term of nine thousand years, after which Ohrmazd will reign and evil will end.
figures.facet: Evil's dominion made CONTRACTUAL: the present age belongs to the Adversary legally, not accidentally — history as a lease that is guaranteed to expire.
figures.provenance: Eznik of Kolb; the 9,000/12,000-year world-scheme is shared with orthodox Pahlavi cosmology (Bundahishn), which Zurvanism re-parents rather than replaces.

figures.gloss: The turning firmament and its zodiac as the dispenser of every human lot — the fatalist strand in which wealth, station, and even wisdom come by the decree of Time and the stars, not by works.
figures.facet: Cosmic order curdled into fatalism: where orthodox Zoroastrianism stakes everything on free moral choice, the Zurvanite current lets the wheel of Time decide — the heresy's deepest break with the parent.
figures.provenance: Fatalist passages of the Menog-i Khrad; Zaehner's analysis of 'Zurvanite fatalism.' How far this strand was distinctly Zurvanite rather than broadly late-Sasanian is part of the sect debate — flagged.

figures.gloss: Grand vizier of Sasanian Persia under three kings (Yazdegerd I, Bahram V, and Yazdegerd II), whose letter to the Armenians on the eve of the 451 Vardanank revolt — preserved in Elishe's History of Vardan — opens by expounding Zurvan's sacrifice and the birth of the twin spirits as official state theology, before demanding Armenian conversion.
figures.facet: The one named body behind the doctrine: Zurvanism is otherwise reconstructed entirely from hostile outside witnesses, but Mihr Narseh is a real historical official caught, in his own words, teaching the twin-birth myth as Sasanian orthodoxy.
figures.provenance: Elishe (Ełišē), History of Vardan and the Armenian War (5th c., surviving in a somewhat later redaction); the letter is the fullest primary-source (if hostilely transmitted) statement of Zurvanite doctrine as state policy.

figures.gloss: Az, 'Concupiscence' or 'Greed' — a devouring demoness of insatiable appetite who, in Zaehner's reconstruction of the Zurvanite strand of Pahlavi cosmology (chiefly the Zand-i Wahman Yasn), threatens at the end of days to consume the very material creation she has fed on throughout history.
figures.facet: Matter's own hunger cast as the final enemy: where Angra Mainyu is a moral chooser of the Lie, Az is appetite itself personified — a rare case where the tradition's evil is not a will opposed to the good but a need that never stops wanting.
figures.provenance: Zaehner, Zurvan: A Zoroastrian Dilemma (1955), reading passages of the Zand-i Wahman Yasn and the Skand-gumanig Wizar as Zurvanite. Her prominence as a specifically ZURVANITE (rather than generally late-Zoroastrian) figure is Zaehner's own reconstruction, contested by Shaked and de Jong — flagged as scholarly-reconstructed rather than a securely attested sectarian doctrine.

edges.note: GOLD SEAM: The Zurvanite twin-myth is direct exegesis of Yasna 30.3, where the two primal Spirits are 'twins' — the heresy grows from the parent scripture's own unanswered question: twins of whom?
GOLD SEAM: Zurvanism demotes the uncreated Wise Lord into a created son of Time — orthodoxy's supreme god becomes one of two brothers, a reading the Pahlavi books polemicize against.

GOLD SEAM: In Middle Persian Manichaica (Turfan fragments) Mani's Father of Greatness is rendered 'Zurwan' — the Apostle of Light borrowed the Iranian time-god's name to preach his supreme deity to Persia.
GOLD SEAM: Cumont read the lion-headed Aion of the Mithraea as Zurvan; modern scholarship doubts the identification — kept as a debated structural rhyme of boundless Time enthroned, flagged.

GOLD SEAM: Unaging Time as the first principle from which the cosmos unfolds — influence between Iran and the Orphic theogonies has been argued in both directions (Zaehner, West) but never proven; structural kinship only.
GOLD SEAM: Fate as the cosmic decree no one escapes — but the Stoic sage assents to eimarmene as reason, while the Zurvanite fatalist merely submits to the turning sphere.
GOLD SEAM: The fixed term of the cosmic battle ending in the renovation is the shared Zoroastrian clock — Zurvanism keeps orthodoxy's ending intact while re-parenting its combatants.
Both name Time itself, not a rival deity, as the one force the highest power must structure creation to withstand — a structural rhyme across unrelated systems, no contact implied.
Mihr Narseh's letter to the Armenians is the fullest surviving primary-source statement of the Zurvan myth as taught doctrine — the historical mouthpiece and the god he preached.

The same-named demoness of devouring appetite appears in both Zurvanite-reconstructed Pahlavi cosmology and Manichaean Middle Persian texts; the direction of borrowing between Sasanian-era Zoroastrian and Manichaean demonology is debated, so kept as an attested historical link rather than a proven descent.
The Zurvanite myth names its dark twin outright: Ahriman is the being conceived from Zurvan's momentary doubt, tearing his way from the womb first to seize the promised kingship.

dossier.essence: An extinct theological current within Zoroastrianism, prominent in Sasanian Persia, that exalted Zurvan — boundless, neuter Time — as the primordial godhead and father of both Ohrmazd and Ahriman. It is known almost entirely through hostile witnesses: Armenian and Syriac Christian polemicists, Greek notices, and later Muslim heresiographers; whether it was ever an organized sect rather than a speculation inside the orthodox priesthood is itself contested.
dossier.cosmos: Before anything existed, Infinite Time sacrificed for a thousand years to obtain a son; Ohrmazd was conceived of the sacrifice itself, Ahriman of a single moment of doubt — and Ahriman tore his way out first to claim the kingship Zurvan had vowed to the firstborn. Bound by his word, Zurvan leases the world to the Adversary for nine thousand years, after which Ohrmazd reigns and evil ends — the orthodox Zoroastrian ending kept intact beneath a re-parented beginning. A fatalist strand associated with the current assigns each human lot to the turning sphere (spihr) and its stars rather than to works.
dossier.practice: No distinctly Zurvanite rite, priesthood, or community is directly attested; adherents presumably kept ordinary Zoroastrian observance, and the current's footprint is doctrinal rather than liturgical. Its high-water mark is the mid-5th-century Sasanian court — the creed put in the mouth of the grandee Mihr Narseh by the Armenian historian Elishe, and the myth refuted at length by Eznik of Kolb in the same decades.
dossier.canon: No Zurvanite scripture survives; the myth is reconstructed from outside witnesses — chiefly Eznik of Kolb's Refutation of the Sects (c. 440s), Theodore bar Konai's Scholion, and al-Shahrastani — with fatalist echoes inside Pahlavi books such as the Menog-i Khrad, while the Avesta itself knows Zurvan only as a minor time-divinity.
dossier.reflects: The drive to heal a dualism into monism — the demand that even two warring co-eternals have one parent, met here at the price of making Time, not goodness, ultimate.
sources: R. C. Zaehner, 'Zurvan: A Zoroastrian Dilemma' (1955)
Shaul Shaked, 'Dualism in Transformation: Varieties of Religion in Sasanian Iran' (1994)
Albert de Jong, 'Traditions of the Magi: Zoroastrianism in Greek and Latin Literature' (1997)

links.cite: R. C. Zaehner, 'Zurvan: A Zoroastrian Dilemma' (1955)
Shaul Shaked, 'Dualism in Transformation: Varieties of Religion in Sasanian Iran' (1994)
Albert de Jong, 'Traditions of the Magi: Zoroastrianism in Greek and Latin Literature' (1997)
